EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong storm and front moved into the Bering Sea Sunday producing strong east wind and snow resulting in a blizzard across the Pribilof Islands.
A front moved into the eastern Bering Sea Friday afternoon producing strong east wind and snow across Saint George Island that resulted in a blizzard.
An intense North Pacific storm produced strong wind and snow across the Eastern Aleutians an Pribilof Island resulting in a blizzard.
An intense North Pacific storm produced strong wind and snow across the Eastern Aleutians an Pribilof Island resulting in a blizzard.
A storm and its associated front moved into the Gulf of Alaska Friday. Strong wind combined with snow in advance of the front produced blizzard conditions across Kodiak Island.
Strong east wind and snow combined to produce a blizzard across the Pribilof Islands.
A storm moved into eastern Cook Inlet producing strong wind and snow resulting in a blizzard over the southern Peninsula.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter from Nikiski reported wind speeds of 39-57 mph along with 2 to 3 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low moved south of the Aleutians producing strong east wind along with snow from the Kuskokwim Delta to the Pribilof Islands. The combination of wind and snow resulted in Blizzard conditions for these regions.

A strong low moved south of the Aleutians producing strong east wind along with snow from the Kuskokwim Delta to the Pribilof Islands. The combination of wind and snow resulted in Blizzard conditions for these regions.
Strong east to northeast wind combined with snow resulted in a Blizzard in the Pribilof Islands.

CZ_NAME: INNER CHANNELS FROM KUPREANOF ISLAND TO ETOLIN ISLAND
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the twenty-ninth the arctic front was over the Panhandle with high pressure centered over the Yukon. This pattern caused some cold outflow through the Stikine river gap. On the thirtieth a frontal system moved near Dixon Entrance which caused warm moist air to briefly move over the arctic air in the Inner Channels.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The trained spotter at the forest service office in Petersburg measured 6.5 inches of new snow that fell from 0700 to 1220 on the thirtieth. Another spotter in Petersburg measured 7.5 inches of new snow from 0700 to 1500 on the thirtieth.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm in the eastern Bering Sea produced strong east wind along with snow through Portage Valley that resulted in a blizzard.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The low visibility coupled with strong wind and snow reported resulted in concluding visibilities in Portage Valley were near zero.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense storm in the Bering Sea produced snow and strong wind over the Kuskokwim Delta that resulted in a Blizzard across the region.

An intense storm moved into the Bering Sea on the 18th. High wind in advance of this storm blew through portions of the Aleutians, then moved to the Bristol Bay Coast and eventually hit the south central portion of Alaska on the 20th. A secondary front wrapped around this storm and produced another shot of high wind along the Anchorage Hillside on the 21st.

An intense storm moved across the Aleutians into the Bering Sea producing hurricane force wind along the Aleutians then Blizzard conditions across the Pribilof Islands to the Bering Sea Coast on the 23rd and 24th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Buoy 46070 reported a gust to 74 kt followed by seas to 47.9 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense storm moved across the Aleutians into the Bering Sea producing hurricane force wind along the Aleutians then Blizzard conditions across the Pribilof Islands to the Bering Sea Coast on the 23rd and 24th.
A low off of Prince William Sound produced snow and gusty north wind over the Western Prince William Sound region that resulted in blizzard conditions in northern Resurrection Bay.

Strong high pressure over the North Slope and moderate low pressure over the Gulf of Alaska created moderate winds near the Alaska-Canada border, and with cold temperatures this resulted in a period of Extreme Wind Chill, as reported by the Remote Automated Weather Station at Eagle, where the Wind Chill reach -70 F. Sustained Wind speeds were 20 to 30 mph during the event. Event continued into March.

An intense hurricane force storm moved into the western Bering Sea Sunday January 13th. As the storm approached the region the western Aleutians, hurricane force wind blew through the Aleutian Islands. Snow combined with the strong wind created blizzard conditions in the eastern Aleutians and Pribilof Islands.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Satellite estimated wind of 60 KT with this storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense hurricane force storm moved into the western Bering Sea Sunday January 13th. As the storm approached the region the western Aleutians, hurricane force wind blew through the Aleutian Islands. Snow combined with the strong wind created blizzard conditions in the eastern Aleutians and Pribilof Islands.

An intense hurricane force storm moved into the western Bering Sea Sunday January 13th. As the storm approached the region the western Aleutians, hurricane force wind blew through the Aleutian Islands. Snow combined with the strong wind created blizzard conditions in the eastern Aleutians and Pribilof Islands.
A strong storm produced strong wind and snow resulting in a blizzard across the Pribilof Islands Monday, January 14th.

An intense storm moved across the Aleutians into the Bering Sea producing hurricane force wind along the Aleutians then Blizzard conditions across the Pribilof Islands to the Bering Sea Coast on the 23rd and 24th.

An intense storm moved across the Aleutians into the Bering Sea producing hurricane force wind along the Aleutians then Blizzard conditions across the Pribilof Islands to the Bering Sea Coast on the 23rd and 24th.
A deep intense low moved into the Western Bering Sea while pushing a front across the western Aleutians. Strong wind in advance of this front occurred in the western Aleutians.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Based upon observations from Shemya and the surface analysis, the wind is estimated to have gusted to 80 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low crossed the central Aleutians December 17th and 18th. Strong north to northwest wind around the west side of the low coupled with snow resulted in a blizzard in Adak.

A large strong high pressure system over interior Alaska coupled with a strong low in the Gulf of Alaska produced the typical strong gap wind through the Chugach Mountains. This is a data void region and wind speed is estimated from the pressure and temperature gradients and satellite imagery.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Wind speed is estimated from the pressure and temperature gradient and satellite imagery.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong Gulf of Alaska low combined with building high pressure in the Bering Sea produced strong northwest wind across Kodiak Island. The peak wind was 91 mph during this event.
A strong occluded front brought a period of blizzard conditions to Kotzebue. The wind frequently gusted to 45 mph and the visibility was reduced to one quarter mile or less in snow and blowing snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A strong occluded front brought a period of blizzard conditions to Kotzebue. The wind frequently gusted to 45 mph and the visibility was reduced to one quarter mile or less in snow and blowing snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak low in the Gulf of Alaska coupled with a strengthening high in the Copper River Basin produced strong gap wind through the channeled terrain of the Chugach Mountains. The strong gusty wind produced blowing snow due to the re suspension of the freshly fallen snow that resulted in a blizzard in the Thompson Pass area.
A cold airmass combined with winds around 15 mph over the Western Arctic Coast produced Extreme Wind Chills, as reported by the Point Lay AWOS, where the Wind Chill dropped to -66 F.
A strong low offshore of the Kuskokwim Delta coast produced snow and strong south wind that resulted in a blizzard along the Kuskokwim Delta coast.
A pair of strong storms south of the Aleutians and Alaska Peninsula produced strong northeast wind and snow across the Pribilof Islands that resulted in a Blizzard.
A strong storm and front moved to the central and eastern Bering Sea producing strong wind and snow that resulted in a blizzard across the Pribilof Islands.

A low pressure center moving north over southwest Alaska and its associated occlusion moving over Norton Sound likely produced blizzard conditions for a brief time on the 15th, as reported by the Unalakleet AWOS.
A storm over south central Alaska produced strong north wind and bands of snow showers over the Kuskokwim Delta resulting in localized blizzard conditions.
Strong northwest wind and snow produced blizzard conditions across portions of Kodiak Island due to a storm in the Gulf of Alaska.
Strong northwest wind and snow across the Alaska Peninsula resulted in a blizzard.
An intense north Pacific storm pushed a front into the eastern Bering Sea. Strong wind and snow in advance of the front produced a blizzard in the Kuskokwim Delta.
A Bering Sea storm produce strong wind and snow across the Kuskokwim Delta resulting in a blizzard.
A strong low in the eastern Bering Sea produced snow and gusty northwest wind that resulted in a blizzard in the Pribilof Islands.
A strong low in the Gulf of Alaska produced strong gusty northwest wind across the Alaska Peninsula. The wind accelerated through the channeled terrain to around 75 to 85 mph.

A strong low crossed the Alaska Peninsula May 23rd producing strong east wind in advance of the associated front. Peak gusts were reported to 84 mph in Naknek that resulted in damage to some roofs, the Naknek trading post and the Peterson Point fish plant.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: People in Naknek reported wind gusts to 84 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Event continued from July. A 980 mb low pressure center moved counter-clockwise across the southern Beaufort Sea during 29th through Aug 1st, bringing several periods of  west winds 30 to 50 mph across the Arctic Coast of Alaska. This caused a combination of high surf and elevated ocean water levels which destroyed the seawall and threatened 3 structures at the village of Wainwright on the western coast, and damaged the seawall and half of the airstrip at the village of Kaktovik, on the far eastern coast. On Aug 1, a father and his 13-year-old son in an open skiff perished in Camden Bay under strong winds as they were boating from their camp to Kaktovik. Property Damage Estimated at $7 million for Wainwright Seawall; $800,000 for Kaktovik Seawall and Airstrip.
A moderate pressure gradient over the Lisburne Peninsula strengthened on the 23rd creating high winds at Point Hope. The AWOS reported gusts to 56 knots (64 mph).

Strong high pressure over the northern Chukchi Sea and the Beaufort Sea created cold temperatures over the North Slope, and winds from 15 to 25 mph created Extreme Wind Chills as reported by the Point Lay AWOS, where the coldest wind chill was -62 F.

CZ_NAME: WRN TANANA VLY WRN YUKON VLY
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Southwesterly flow over central Alaska moved a warm front over the area creating heavy snowfall. Manley Hot Springs reported 9.5 inches and Eureka Dome Summit on the Elliott highway Milepost 119 received 12.0 inches during the event.

An intense storm moved into the Bering Sea on the 18th. High wind in advance of this storm blew through portions of the Aleutians, then moved to the Bristol Bay Coast and eventually hit the south central portion of Alaska on the 20th. The strong southeast flow pushed ample moisture into the Alaska Range dumping 14 inches of snow in that region on the 20th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A significant cold snap developed across interior Alaska on December 27th and continued into January.  A cold upper level low across Siberia moved into northern Alaska on December 26th, and remained in place through the end of the month.  Although no new temperature records were established, it brought the first widespread significant cold to interior Alaska.  Temperatures at the Fairbanks International Airport dropped to 40 below on the 28th, and reached a low of 43 degrees on the 31st.  The cold temperatures produced significant ice fog in Fairbanks, and the visibilities were frequently reduced to 1/4 mile or less in Fairbanks on New Year's Eve.  The coldest temperatures were observed on the Yukon Flats, where temperatures dropped into the 50s below on New Year's Eve.||Here are some of the coldest temperatures that were observed through December 31st:||Zone 218: |Arctic Village: 49 below||Zone 219: |Bettles: 45 below||Zone 220: |Chalkyitsik: 58 below|Beaver Creek: 57 below|Circle Hot Springs: 55 below|Fort Yukon: 54 below|Central: 50 below||Zone 221: |Nenana: 44 below||Zone 222: |North Pole KJNP: 55 below|Fairbanks International Airport: 43 below||Zone 223: |Delta Junction: 44 below||Zone 224: |O'Brien Creek: 57 below|Tok: 52 below|Chicken: 52 below|Eagle: 50 below|Northway: 49 below||Zone 225: |Denali Park HQ: 34 below||Zone 226: |Dry Creek: 50 below|Chisana: 47 below||All of these low temperatures were observed on either December 30th or the 31st.

A strong north Pacific storm moved across Kodiak Island producing strong gap wind through Portage Valley. The wind peaked at 75 mph at the Portage Lake Visitor Center early Thursday morning.
A Gulf of Alaska storm moved to the north gulf coast producing snow and wind in Thompson Pass that resulted in a blizzard.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: DOT in Thompson Pass reported zero visibility through the pass.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low moved into the Gulf of Alaska southeast of Kodiak Island. Strong northeast wind combined with snow produce blizzard conditions over Kodiak Island.

An intense low south of Kodiak Island produced snow and strong north wind across the Alaska Peninsula resulting in blizzard conditions. The peak measured wind was 82 KT at King Cove with visibility near zero in the early afternoon of the 28th.
A strong low packing hurricane force wind moved through the Aleutians producing winds gusting to around 74 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Buoy 46071 recorded a gust to 64 KT.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very unstable atmospheric conditions in southwest Alaska produced severe thunderstorms in the Bethel area the evening of July 5th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Kipnuk observed gusts to 55 mph. Estimated wind gusts to exceed 50 KT.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Persistent southwest flow produced moderate to heavy rain along the Alaska Range Thursday and Friday. The rainfall along with high snow melt run off resulted in high water and minor flooding along the Yentna River. Homes along the river had water in their yards.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The river gage along the Yentna River at Lake Creek was .4 ft below flood stage. Reports from residents along the river were of water in their yards without any damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong pressure gradient between a 1056 mb high across interior Alaska and a 998 mb low that moved off the eastern Russian Coast |and into the Chukchi Sea produced high winds on Saint Lawrence Island, the Bering Strait and along the Western Arctic Coast. Wind gusts of 57 to 64 kts (66 to 74 mph) were observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A strong pressure gradient between a 1056 mb high across interior Alaska and a 998 low in the Chukchi Sea produced strong winds along the Western Arctic Coast. Wind gusts to 74 mph were observed at the Cape Lisburne AWOS and 61 mph at Point Lay AWOS.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense hurricane force storm moved into the western Bering Sea Sunday January 13th. As the storm approached the region the western Aleutians, hurricane force wind blew through the Aleutian Islands. Snow combined with the strong wind created blizzard conditions in the eastern Aleutians and Pribilof Islands.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Satellite estimated wind indicates sustained wind of 60 KT with this storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense low in the eastern Gulf of Alaska and strong high pressure over the Bering Sea produced strong northwest wind and snow across the Alaska Peninsula that resulted in a blizzard.
Strong north wind produced by a strong low in the Gulf of Alaska resulted in re suspension of snow. The resulting blowing snow produce blizzard conditions through Thompson Pass.

Cold air moved into Northern Southeast Alaska on 10/27 and 10/28 due to a low level north wind. Warm moist air was forced aloft over this colder air as a storm center moved into the Western Gulf which caused high coastal winds and locally heavy snow inland.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Haines COOP observer measured 13 inches of new snowfall for 24 hours ending 0800 AKST 10/29.

CZ_NAME: SALISBURY SOUND TO CAPE FAIRWEATHER COASTAL AREA
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm force low developed well southwest of the Panhandle deepening to 967 MB in the central Gulf of Alaska late Tuesday night 10/21. A strong associated front moved onto the coast also in the night of 10/21. The low dissipated in the central Gulf of Alaska on Wednesday 10/22. The strongest gusts recorded were 78 MPH.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Although the wind speeds at Elfin Cove were not significant on 10/22, Cape Spencer observed a peak wind of 74 KT at 0400 AKST on 10/22 and very strong winds continued throughout the day there. It is estimated that the winds along headlands and channeled terrain were around 75 MPH during this event.

CZ_NAME: CAPE DECISION TO SALISBURY SOUND COASTAL AREA
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm force low developed well southwest of the Panhandle deepening to 967 MB in the central Gulf of Alaska late Tuesday night 10/21. A strong associated front moved onto the coast also in the night of 10/21. The low dissipated in the central Gulf of Alaska on Wednesday 10/22. The strongest gusts recorded were 78 MPH.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sitka ASOS had a peak wind of 60 MPH at 0153 AKST 10/22. Numerous gusts were observed there in the 50 MPH range. NCEP Analyses showed such strong surface pressure gradients that headland winds could have been as high as 80 MPH on 10/22.

CZ_NAME: DIXON ENTRANCE TO CAPE DECISION COASTAL AREA
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm force low developed well southwest of the Panhandle deepening to 967 MB in the central Gulf of Alaska late Tuesday night 10/21. A strong associated front moved onto the coast also in the night of 10/21. The low dissipated in the central Gulf of Alaska on Wednesday 10/22. The strongest gusts recorded were 78 MPH.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hydaburg AWOS measured several wind gusts at 49 KT during the evening of 10/21. Gusts were estimated around Craig at 60 KT on the night of 10/21. Klawock Airport ASOS also had a measured gust to 49 KT at midnight 10/22.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm force low developed well southwest of the Panhandle deepening to 967 MB in the central Gulf of Alaska late Tuesday night 10/21. A strong associated front moved onto the coast also in the night of 10/21. The low dissipated in the central Gulf of Alaska on Wednesday 10/22. The strongest gusts recorded were 78 MPH.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotters at Thorne Bay and Meyers Chuck estimated 65 KT during the night of 10/21 into the morning of 10/22. Marine reports showed even higher gusts. The Annette ASOS measured a peak wind 55 KT at 2153 AKST on 10/21. No damage was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm force low developed well southwest of the Panhandle deepening to 967 MB in the central Gulf of Alaska late Tuesday night 10/21. A strong associated front moved onto the coast also in the night of 10/21. The low dissipated in the central Gulf of Alaska on Wednesday 10/22. The strongest gusts recorded were 78 MPH.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Based on pressure gradients from NCEP analyses and surface observations in Zone 28, wind speeds in excess of 65 KT occurred on the night of 10/21 for the western areas of the Misty Fjords. No damage was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm force low developed well southwest of the Panhandle deepening to 967 MB in the central Gulf of Alaska late Tuesday night 10/21. A strong associated front moved onto the coast also in the night of 10/21. The low dissipated in the central Gulf of Alaska on Wednesday 10/22. The strongest gusts recorded were 78 MPH.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Skagway ASOS reported a wind gust 53 MPH at 1353 AKST on 10/22. White Pass had estimated gusts to 70 MPH around the same time, and a marine observation, Eldred Rock in Lynn Canal had a peak wind of 61 KT at 1700 AKST on 10/22.

CZ_NAME: WRN TANANA VLY WRN YUKON VLY
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A 958 mb low in the Gulf of Alaska combined with a 1039 mb high across northern Canada to produce a strong pressure gradient across central Alaska.  Wind damage was observed at lake Minchumina, with tree branches down. Wood paneling on a log frame porch on the north side of the lake was blown off on 2 sides and scattered across the yard. Unconfirmed estimated cost of damage was $150.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 958 mb low in the Gulf of Alaska combined with a 1039 mb high across northern Canada to produce a strong pressure gradient across central Alaska. Wind damage was observed at lake Minchumina, with tree branches down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A 998 mb low rapidly tracked across the southern Arctic Ocean on the 15th to Banks Island at 3 am on the 16th.  There was a strong pressure gradient between the low and a 1045 mb high that built in behind the low across the Chukchi Sea.  Snow and strong wind combined to produce blizzard conditions along much of the Beaufort Sea Coast from late in the afternoon on the 15th through the morning hours of the 16th.  The worst conditions were observed at Deadhorse with 12 hours with visibilities less than one quarter mile in snow and blowing snow.  The wind frequently gusted to 50 mph, and a peak wind gust of 52 kts (60 mph) was observed at the Deadhorse ASOS. Although the visibility never dropped below one half mile at the Barter Island AWOS, the wind gusted as high as 66 kts (76 mph). Local law enforcement confirmed that blizzard conditions did occur.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 998 mb low rapidly tracked across the southern Arctic Ocean on the 15th to Banks Island at 3 am on the 16th. There was a strong pressure gradient between the low and a 1045 mb high that built in behind the low across the Chukchi Sea. Snow and strong wind combined to produce blizzard conditions along much of the Beaufort Sea Coast from late in the afternoon on the 15th through the morning hours of the 16th. The worst conditions were observed at Deadhorse with 12 hours with visibilities less than one quarter mile in snow and blowing snow. The wind frequently gusted to 50 mph, and a peak wind gust of 60 mph was observed at the Deadhorse ASOS.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong pressure gradient between a 1056 mb high across interior Alaska and a 998 mb low that moved off the eastern Russian Coast |and into the Chukchi Sea produced high winds on Saint Lawrence Island, the Bering Strait and along the Western Arctic Coast. Wind gusts of 57 to 64 kts (66 to 74 mph) were observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A strong pressure gradient between a 1056 mb high across interior Alaska and a 998 mb low in the Chukchi Sea produced a period of high winds along the Bering Strait Coast as well as on Saint Lawrence Island.  The strongest wind gusts were observed at the Savoonga AWOS (69 mph), the Wales AWIS (66 mph) and at the Teller AWIS (69 mph).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A 980 mb low 350 miles south of Saint Lawrence Island on the afternoon of the 4th weakened to 1002 mb near Kodiak Island during the afternoon of the 6th.  A moist flow of air from the Bering Sea brought a prolonged period of snowfall to the Bettles area.  A total of 6.7 inches of snow was observed from 1900AKST on the 4th through 1400AKST on the 6th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 980 mb low 350 miles south of Saint Lawrence Island on the afternoon of the 4th weakened to 1002 mb near Kodiak Island on the afternoon of the 6th.  A moist flow of air from the Bering Sea brought a prolonged period of snowfall to the Bettles area.  A total of 6.7 inches of snow was observed from 7 pm on the 4th through 2 pm on the 6th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system over the western Bering Sea created blizzard conditions over the Bering Strait during the afternoon of the 14th. Blizzard conditions likely occurred at the village of Wales for 8 hours, as reported by the AWIS. Occasional wind gusts to near 52 kts (60 mph) were reported by the Wales AWIS during the afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system over the western Bering Sea likely produced blizzard conditions during the afternoon and early evening hours of the 14th at village of Wales. The Wales AWIS reported sustained wind speeds between 30 and 45 mph with a peak gust of 50 kt (59 mph).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong front moved into the Bering Sea coast producing strong wind and snow that resulted in a Blizzard Christmas eve and Christmas day.

Ahead of a strong occluded front a period of very strong wind was observed at Wales along the Bering Strait coast.  Sustained winds of 50 to 60 mph were observed with frequent gusts to 70 mph and a peak wind gust of 68 kts (78 mph).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ahead of a strong occluded front a period of very strong wind was observed at Wales along the Bering Strait coast.  Sustained winds of 50 to 60 mph were observed with frequent gusts to 70 mph and a peak wind gust of 68 kts (78 mph).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A 958 mb low in the Gulf of Alaska produced a strong pressure gradient that produced high winds across the Unalakleet Valley.  A peak wind gust of 59 kts (69 mph) was observed at Unalakleet.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 958 mb low in the Gulf of Alaska produced a strong pressure gradient that caused high winds across the Unalakleet Valley. Frequent gusts to 60 to 65 mph were observed at the Unalakleet AWOS, with a peak wind gust of 69 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong pressure gradient between high pressure over the North Slope and a low in the Gulf of Alaska produced strong winds of 50 mph with local gusts to 61 kts (70 mph) observed at the Koyukuk National Wildlife Refuge RAWS.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust of 61 kts (70 mph) was observed at the Koyukuk National Wildlife Refuge RAWS at 6:18 am on the 9th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong pressure gradient between a 1045 mb high in the Yukon and a 996 mb low in the Gulf of Anadyr produced a period of high winds along the Bering Strait Coast.  The strongest winds were observed at Wales.  A peak wind gust of 60 kts (69 mph) was observed at the Wales AWIS.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A strong pressure gradient between a 1045 mb high in the Yukon and a 996 mb low in the Gulf of Anadyr produced a period of high winds along the Bering Strait Coast.  The strongest winds were observed at Wales.  A peak wind gust of 69 mph was observed at the Wales AWIS.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong surface trough extended from a 980 mb low near the Bering Strait along the Arctic Coast and produced blizzard conditions for about 10 hours in Deadhorse.  An east wind of 35 to 45 mph with occasional gusts to 48 kts (55 mph) were observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A strong surface trough extended from a 980 mb low near the Bering Strait along the Arctic Coast and produced blizzard conditions for about 10 hours in Deadhorse.  An east wind of 35 to 45 mph with occasional gusts to 55 mph were observed. The visibility was one quarter mile or less in blowing snow from 5 pm on the 9th through 3 am on the 10th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of high pressure in the interior and a low in the northern Gulf of Alaska created a tight pressure gradient over the North Gulf Coast of Alaska. The combined strong pressure gradient and cold air out flow from the interior along with a one and a half feet of new snow in the pass resulted in high winds over 100 mph re suspending the snow creating a blizzard in Thompson Pass.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Estimated wind gusts in the Copper River Delta based on wind in Thompson Pass and Valdez characterizing the magnitude of the gap wind.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of high pressure in the interior and a low in the northern Gulf of Alaska created a tight pressure gradient over the North Gulf Coast of Alaska. The combined strong pressure gradient and cold air out flow from the interior along with a one and a half feet of new snow in the pass resulted in high winds over 100 mph re suspending the snow creating a blizzard in Thompson Pass.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Blizzard conditions were created throughout Thompson Pass as winds to 111 mph picked up and drifted snow through the area. Less than 1/4 mile visibilities and white-out conditions were reported from DOT officials as early as 4 am on the 26th of Jan. These conditions persisted through approximately 7pm on the 26th. Strong winds continued to effect the area as late as 11pm but visibilities had improved to greater than 1 mile as early as 7pm.

Although the arctic front had moved over the northern Panhandle, cold air remained over the southern part of SE Alaska on the evening of 12/27. A low pressure system off the eastern Gulf coast developed a front that pushed warm moist air over the cold air which caused heavy snow on 12/28. Storm total snow accumulations ranged from 4.6 to 17.8 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Ketchikan area got buried by persistent snow on 12/28. Storm totals from spotters ranged from a low of 8.0 inches to 17.8 inches of new snow on that day.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A major snowstorm buried SE Alaska from the night of 12/25 persisting to the early morning of 12/27. A secondary gale force low developed in the western Gulf of Alaska on the afternoon of 12/25, and it became the main surface low in the central Gulf on the morning of 12/26. This system caused a strong front to move over the Panhandle on the 12/26 with plenty of moisture where it stalled on the 27th and 28th. Storm total snwo accumulations ranged from one to two feet during this storm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Glacier Bay cooperative observer measured 6.5 inches of new snow for 24 hours ending at 0800 AKST on 10/26. This snow fell the previous night. Glacier Bay had 10.0 inches new on 12/27 ending at 0800. Gustavus airport AWOS showed that the heaviest snow ended on the afternoon of 10/26.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A north pacific storm pushed a front across the eastern Aleutians and western Alaska Peninsula February 12th. Strong wind and snow associated with this front produced blizzard conditions across the region during the afternoon and evening of the 12th. The low then moved along the south side of the Alaska Peninsula producing strong northeast to northwest wind from the Bering Sea Coast to the Pribilof islands to the Alaska Peninsula and Eastern Aleutians.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spotter report from King Cove of white out conditions and drifting.

CZ_NAME: CAPE FAIRWEATHER TO CAPE SUCKLING COASTAL AREA
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Arctic High pressure remained over the eastern Yukon on the night of 12/8 which supported a stationary arctic front over the northern Panhandle. This combined with a 958 MB storm force low that moved into the western Gulf of Alaska on on the evening of 12/8 to cause both high wind and heavy snow for much of SE Alaska. Rare blizzard conditions persisted during the nighttime hours into 12/9 over the Klondike Highway and Haines areas during this storm. Storm totals of snow ranged from around 9 inches to 18 inches. The highest wind estimate was around 75 MPH.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In the middle of a significant snow storm, the Cape Suckling buoy indicated a strong east wind for the western part of the area starting around 1500 AKST on 12/8. Several gusts to 52 KT were observed there and the NCEP analysis at 2100 AKST showed that strong winds did occur over land west of Icy Bay.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A 1047 MB arctic high pressure cell over the Yukon on 12/2 slowly weakened and moved southeastward through Friday, 12/5. A planetary scale storm force low moved into Bristol Bay on 12/4 which caused a series of fronts to move over SE Alaska from the Gulf through 12/5. Storm totals were from 9.6 inches to up to 2 feet of new snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow began at the Gustavus Airport around midnight 12/4. The Glacier Bay coop observer measured 11.0 inches new snow for 24 hours ending at 0800 AKST 12/4. All of this snowfall was after midnight. The Gustavus coop observer had 7.2 inches ending at 0700 AKST 12/4.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong, cold arctic high pressure remained over the Yukon and northern British Columbia on the evening of 12/22. Local outflow winds developed on the evening of 12/20 into the morning of 12/21 for areas in the northern Panhandle as a moderate low pressure system moved to the Queen Charlotte Islands. Estimated gusts to 75 MPH were the strongest winds encountered during this storm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Frequent wind gusts 60-75 MPH occurred late on the night of 12/20 into the daytime hours 12/21 at the Skagway Airport ASOS. No damage was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A 996mb low in the Northern Gulf of Alaska, combined with a 1026mb high pressure ridge over the interior, created a 24mb pressure difference between Fairbanks and Valdez. These two surface features combined with a passing upper level trough of low pressure, as well as cold air advection, provided the instability for steady snow and strong northerly winds across the zone. Orientation of the surface pressure gradient (West/Southwest to East/Northeast) was also conducive to strong terrain enhanced winds across Northeast Prince William Sound.

Surface 982 Mb low located in the northern Gulf of Alaska combined with high pressure situated over the interior provided a tight pressure gradient over Northeast Prince William Sound. Cold air advection, and orientation of gradients produced a high wind event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: During the morning of February 7th, the RWIS sensor at Mile post 26, recorded Gusts to 84 mph.

A 980 mb low moved north over the southeastern Bering Sea on the 21st, to Nome at 9am on the 22nd and to Barrow 9pm on the 22nd, then moved northeast approaching Banks Island 3pm on the 23rd. This system produced blizzard conditions over portions of western Alaska and the North Slope, and Heavy snow in the Brooks Range.|Blizzard Conditions:|Zone 201: likely at Atqasuk AWSS; highest gust 50 knots (59 mph).|Zone 203: occurred at Deadhorse. Maximum wind gust to 54 knots (62 mph).|Zone 204: likely occurred at Barter Island (Kaktovik). Though the AWOS was not reporting, nearby Komakuk Beach in Canada and Deadhorse both indicated blizzard conditions. Komakuk Beach highest gust was 54 knots (62 mph).|Zone 209: occurred at Kotzebue, and likely at Selawik AWOS, highest gust 49 knots (56 mph).|Zone 210: likely at Deering, highest gust 46 knots (56 mph).|Zone 211: Occurred at Nome, and likely at Golovin; each had highest gust of 49 knots (56 mph). At Nome, power was lost to various sections north of town as power lines iced up rapidly in the wet snowfall, falling temperatures, and strong winds. Extensive damage resulted to 13 homes due to frozen/burst water pipes. The City of Nome set up an Incident Command System to organize emergency resources. Volunteer Fire Fighters and EMTs went door-to-door to the neighborhoods north of town to deliver emergency messages and conduct welfare checks. Surface 982 Mb low located in the northern Gulf of Alaska combined with high pressure situated over the interior provided a tight pressure gradient over Northeast Prince William Sound. Cold air advection, and orientation of gradients produced a high wind event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Based upon the surface analysis and reported wind in Thompson Pass and Valdez, it is estimated the wind gusts were at a minimum 75 KT out of the Copper River Delta.

A 970mb low in the Gulf of Alaska moved north to the Kenai Peninsula. As it moved inland snow fell across the mountains and in Thompson Pass.  The front produced strong wind across Valdez into Thompson Pass resulting in a Blizzard. Blizzard conditions also occurred behind this front in the strong west wind and snow over the southern Kenai Peninsula.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Department of Highways workers reported wind speed of 30 to 40 mph and blowing snow that reduced the visibility to less than a quarter mile this morning at 6 A.M.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm moving into the norther Gulf of Alaska pushed warm air over the very cold low lying air over Kodiak Island. Up to 1/8th inch of ice accumulated in Kodiak City.

An strong storm produced rapidly rising pressure from the east coupled with a strong pressure gradient resulting in high wind through Portage Pass and Portage Valley and along the Anchorage Hillside and Western Turnagain Arm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Department of Highways Road Weather Information Site at Mchugh Creek.

A storm moved into Prince William Sound resulting in gusty northwest wind and snow along Cook Inlet producing a blizzard on the Kenai Peninsula.

A cold front moving northeast over interior Alaska created moist southwest flow over the Middle Tanana Valley which enhanced snowfall. Amounts ranged from  6.5 inches to 10.4 inches, with many reports from Spotters, Cooperative observers and National Weather Service employees being in the 7.0 to 8.5 inch range. This was the single biggest snowfall in about 2 years.

High pressure across the Arctic Ocean and low pressure in the Gulf of Alaska combined to produce locally strong east to northeast winds across portions of interior Alaska on the afternoon of the 9th through the evening of the 10th.  Winds of 15 to 30 mph produced areas of blowing snow, and with temperatures between 20 and 30 below the wind chills were as low as 55 below. The 25th running of the Yukon Quest was delayed on its second day because the Steese Highway was closed by high winds and drifting snow at about mile 81.

Arctic High pressure remained over the eastern Yukon on the night of 12/8 which supported a stationary arctic front over the northern Panhandle. This combined with a 958 MB storm force low that moved into the western Gulf of Alaska on on the evening of 12/8 to cause both high wind and heavy snow for much of SE Alaska. Rare blizzard conditions persisted during the nighttime hours into 12/9 over the Klondike Highway and Haines areas during this storm. Storm totals of snow ranged from around 9 inches to 18 inches. The highest wind estimate was around 75 MPH.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Haines Customs had 18.0 inches of new snow for 24 hours ending at 0800 on 12/9 and 7.0 inches ending at at 0800 on 12/10. This storm was also a blizzard with white out conditions, plus blowing and drifting snow in 35 MPH winds.

CZ_NAME: CAPE DECISION TO SALISBURY SOUND COASTAL AREA
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Arctic High pressure remained over the eastern Yukon on the night of 12/8 which supported a stationary arctic front over the northern Panhandle. This combined with a 958 MB storm force low that moved into the western Gulf of Alaska on on the evening of 12/8 to cause both high wind and heavy snow for much of SE Alaska. Rare blizzard conditions persisted during the nighttime hours into 12/9 over the Klondike Highway and Haines areas during this storm. Storm totals of snow ranged from around 9 inches to 18 inches. The highest wind estimate was around 75 MPH.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The outer coast had winds around 60 MPH late on the night of 12/8 and during the early morning hours of 12/9. Although the Sitka airport only had 47 MPH, NCEP analysis showed strong surface pressure gradients which certainly caused headland winds to 60 MPH ahead of the front.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A 1047 MB arctic high pressure cell over the Yukon on 12/2 slowly weakened and moved southeastward through Friday, 12/5. A planetary scale storm force low moved into Bristol Bay on 12/4 which caused a series of fronts to move over SE Alaska from the Gulf through 12/5. Storm totals were from 9.6 inches to up to 2 feet of new snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Hyder coop observer was away for the month of December, 2008. Observations at Stewart, British Columbia, indicated that constant heavy snow fell on the night of 12/4 and all day 12/5. It is estimated that 1 to 2 feet of snow fell during that period.

CZ_NAME: JUNEAU BOROUGH AND NORTHERN ADMIRALTY ISLAND
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong, cold arctic high pressure remained over the Yukon and northern British Columbia on the evening of 12/22. Local outflow winds developed on the evening of 12/20 into the morning of 12/21 for areas in the northern Panhandle as a moderate low pressure system moved to the Queen Charlotte Islands. Estimated gusts to 75 MPH were the strongest winds encountered during this storm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Juneau South Douglas wind profiler measured peak surface winds 60 to to 71 MPH on the morning of 12/21. No damage was reported during this Taku wind type event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Arctic High pressure remained over the eastern Yukon on the night of 12/8 which supported a stationary arctic front over the northern Panhandle. This combined with a 958 MB storm force low that moved into the western Gulf of Alaska on on the evening of 12/8 to cause both high wind and heavy snow for much of SE Alaska. Rare blizzard conditions persisted during the nighttime hours into 12/9 over the Klondike Highway and Haines areas during this storm. Storm totals of snow ranged from around 9 inches to 18 inches. The highest wind estimate was around 75 MPH.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The NWS office in Yakutat measured 5.2 inches of new snowfall during the evening of 12/8 and 4.0 inches of new snow between 0000 and 0600 AKST on 12/9 for a storm total of 9.2 inches in 12 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Arctic High pressure remained over the eastern Yukon on the night of 12/8 which supported a stationary arctic front over the northern Panhandle. This combined with a 958 MB storm force low that moved into the western Gulf of Alaska on on the evening of 12/8 to cause both high wind and heavy snow for much of SE Alaska. Rare blizzard conditions persisted during the nighttime hours into 12/9 over the Klondike Highway and Haines areas during this storm. Storm totals of snow ranged from around 9 inches to 18 inches. The highest wind estimate was around 75 MPH.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In the early morning hours of 12/9, the Klondike Highway had blizzard conditions as extrapolated from the observations at the Skagway Airport. 4 inches of new snow fell on 12/9 at the power plant while frequent winds to 35 MPH occurred toward the pass. By the persistent visibility of 1 mile in snow and blowing snow in town, the Klondike Highway certainly had white out conditions throughout the day on 12/9.

CZ_NAME: JUNEAU BOROUGH AND NORTHERN ADMIRALTY ISLAND
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Arctic High pressure remained over the eastern Yukon on the night of 12/8 which supported a stationary arctic front over the northern Panhandle. This combined with a 958 MB storm force low that moved into the western Gulf of Alaska on on the evening of 12/8 to cause both high wind and heavy snow for much of SE Alaska. Rare blizzard conditions persisted during the nighttime hours into 12/9 over the Klondike Highway and Haines areas during this storm. Storm totals of snow ranged from around 9 inches to 18 inches. The highest wind estimate was around 75 MPH.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Juneau had one of the worst snowstorms of the season on 12/9. The cooperative observer at mile 17 measured 17.8 inches of new snowfall at 0700 AKST on 12/9. Snow did not begin until the evening of 12/8 and heavy snow was not observed at the airport until late. A peak wind of 30 MPH was observed at the airport on 12/9 which had 10.7 inches of new snow. Most recorded amounts were in the 10 to 15 inches range.

The highest wind estimate was around 75 MPH.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Hydaburg seaplane base observed gusts to 51 KT at 2236 AKST on 12/9. Coastal headlands probably had wind speeds to 75 MPH at that time.

CZ_NAME: SALISBURY SOUND TO CAPE FAIRWEATHER COASTAL AREA

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The automated observation at Cape Spencer indicated that quite a wind storm persisted along the outer coast from the night of 12/8 well into 12/10. E wind with gusts estimated at 65 KT shifted to SW then W that Tuesday.

CZ_NAME: CAPE DECISION TO SALISBURY SOUND COASTAL AREA
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On 12/23 arctic high pressure over the Yukon had moved well SE but cold air remained over the Panhandle. A 983 MB storm force low had moved into the central Gulf with a strong front approaching the coast. This caused warm moist air to move over the colder air at the surface causing heavy snow along the outer coast. Storm totals over 12 hours ranged from 7.4 inches to around a foot of new snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Little Port Walter measured 10.2 inches of new snow for 24 hours ending at 1700 AKST on 12/23 and another 6.3 inches on 12/24. No snow measurements were available for Sitka.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A 996mb low in the Northern Gulf of Alaska, combined with a 1026mb high pressure ridge over the interior, created a 24mb pressure difference between Fairbanks and Valdez. These two surface features combined with a passing upper level trough of low pressure, as well as cold air advection, provided the instability for steady snow and strong northerly winds across the zone. Orientation of the surface pressure gradient (West/Southwest to East/Northeast) was also conducive to strong terrain enhanced winds across Northeast Prince William Sound.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At 10:30am on the 2nd. A report from a citizen of Valdez that gusty winds and less than �� visibilities at Alpine Woods (mile post 10)were being witnessed. The 12 mile Road Weather Information System (RWIS) was reporting 32 mph gusts and falling snow with poor visibility on the cameras. In Valdez: Reduced visibilities to 1/4 mile were being reported from NWS employees at WSO Valdez as early as 12pm. In the Pass: Observations started coming in at 6pm with white-out conditions and strong winds to 92 mph occurring at 5:44 pm on the 2nd of February. These conditions persisted through the night until 6am on the 3rd when the last report from Thompson Pass came in.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A 996mb low in the Northern Gulf of Alaska, combined with a 1026mb high pressure ridge over the interior, created a 24mb pressure difference between Fairbanks and Valdez. These two surface features combined with a passing upper level trough of low pressure, as well as cold air advection, provided the instability for steady snow and strong northerly winds across the zone. Orientation of the surface pressure gradient (West/Southwest to East/Northeast) was also conducive to strong terrain enhanced winds across Northeast Prince William Sound.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thompson Pass Road Weather Information site reported peak gusts to 119 KT out of the north. These winds are characteristic of the magnitude of the gap winds through the Chugach Mountains that most likely occurred out of the Copper River Delta.

CZ_NAME: CAPE FAIRWEATHER TO CAPE SUCKLING COASTAL AREA
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A 1047 MB arctic high pressure cell over the Yukon on 12/2 slowly weakened and moved southeastward through Friday, 12/5. A planetary scale storm force low moved into Bristol Bay on 12/4 which caused a series of fronts to move over SE Alaska from the Gulf through 12/5. Storm totals were from 9.6 inches to up to 2 feet of new snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The NWS office in Yakutat measured 2.4 inches of new snow for 24 hours ending at midnight 12/4 and 7.2 inches for 24 hours ending at midnight 12/5. Most of the snow fell between 2100 AKST 12/3 and noon 12/4 for a storm total of 9.6 inches snowfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system moved northeast over Bristol Bay and caused winds to increase over Norton Sound and provide snow to the area, likely creating blizzard conditions at Unalakleet, as reported by the AWOS.

A strong low over Kodiak Island produced snow and gusty North to Northwest wind across the Kuskokwim Delta to the Alaska Peninsula. This resulted in blizzard conditions across both areas.

Cold Air flowing out of the Copper River Basin was funneled through Thompson Pass. The strong outflow wind combined with snow produced blizzard conditions through the pass. Accompanying the blizzard were very cold temperatures producing dangerous wind chill conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple Motorist reported White out conditions while in travel. The D.O.T. worker in the Pass also reported blizzard conditions in Thompson Pass and at Keystone Canyon.

Nighttime skies cleared over much of the Northern Panhandle on the night of October 6th. Freezing temperatures into the mid 20s F were observed for Juneau, Glacier Bay, and Eastern Chichagof Island.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Temperatures went below freezing as measured at Hoonah Airport AWOS during the morning of Tuesday 10/07 between midnight and 9 AM AKST. The minimum temperature recorded was 29F. Outlying areas were estimated to have temperatures in the lower 20s.

CZ_NAME: JUNEAU BOROUGH AND NORTHERN ADMIRALTY ISLAND
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Nighttime skies cleared over much of the Northern Panhandle on the night of October 6th. Freezing temperatures into the mid 20s F were observed for Juneau, Glacier Bay, and Eastern Chichagof Island.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Juneau airport ASOS measured temperatures below freezing from 11 PM AKST on the night of 10/6 through 10 AM AKST 10/7. The minimum temperature recorded was 26F.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On 11/1 the arctic front was stationary over the Haines area. Warm moist air moved over the area aloft. New snowfall ranged over the Haines and the Chilikat Highway from 8.6 to 21 inches in 24 hours ending 0800 AKST on 11/2.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Haines customs measured 21.0 inches of new snowfall for 24 hours ending at 0700 AKST 11/2. Haines cooperative observer downtown measured 8.6 inches of new snowfall for 24 hours ending at 0800 on 11/2.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A 980 mb low 350 miles south of Saint Lawrence Island brought snowfall to the lower Koyukuk and middle Yukon Valleys.  The cooperative observer in Galena observed 7 inches of snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The cooperative observer in Galena observed 7 inches of snow with a water content of 32 hundredths of an inch from 1800AKST on the 3rd through 1800AKST on the 4th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A 980 mb low 350 miles south of Saint Lawrence Island combined with a 1033 mb high across eastern Russia to produce a period of very strong wind on Saint Lawrence Island.  The wind frequently gusted to 53 kts (61 mph) at the Gambell AWOS from 1435AKST through 1930AKST.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 979 mb low 350 miles south of Saint Lawrence Island combined with a 1033 mb ridge across Eastern Russia to produce a period of very strong wind on Saint Lawrence Island. A peak wind gust of 53 kts(61 mph) was reported at the Gambell AWOS on Saint Lawrence Island.

CZ_NAME: S. SLOPES OF ERN BROOKS RANGE
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong pressure gradient between a high pressure over the Beafort Sea and a low in the Gulf of Alaska produced sustained winds of 30 to 40 mph with local gusts to 50 kts (60 mph) reported by the cooperative weather observer at Wiseman on the evening of the 9th: the observer estimated stronger winds nearby.  An 8 to 10 inch diameter tree was blown over in the yard.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds developed during the afternoon of the 9th. Wind speeds of 30 to 45 mph were observed with a peak gust estimated at 60 mph.  The strong wind knocked down an 8 to 10 inch diameter tree and knocked out the microwave telephone system in the area.

CZ_NAME: CAPE DECISION TO SALISBURY SOUND COASTAL AREA
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Cold air moved into Northern Southeast Alaska on 10/27 and 10/28 due to a low level north wind. Warm moist air was forced aloft over this colder air as a storm center moved into the Western Gulf which caused high coastal winds and locally heavy snow inland.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sitka airport ASOS had peak winds 49 KT around 1600 to 1700 AKST on 10/28. NCEP analyses indicted that strong pressure gradients at that time caused winds across headlands in excess of 70 MPH.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Nighttime skies cleared over much of the Northern Panhandle on the night of October 6th. Freezing temperatures into the mid 20s F were observed for Juneau, Glacier Bay, and Eastern Chichagof Island.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Gustavus airport ASOS measured temperatures below freezing between 11 PM AKST Monday October 6th and 10 AM Tuesday October 7th. The lowest temperature recorded was 26F.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense storm moved across the Aleutians into the eastern Bering Sea March 28th and 29th. Strong wind and snow ahead of the associated front produced blizzard conditions across the Pribilof Islands and along the Bering Sea coast March 29th.

CZ_NAME: JUNEAU BOROUGH AND NORTHERN ADMIRALTY ISLAND
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the eleventh a storm force low approached from the south which moved onto the coast near Cape Decision at 981 MB that afternoon. This was rapidly followed by another storm moving to near Sitka at 984 MB on the afternoon of the thirteenth. This center moved northward into the interior on the morning of the fourteenth followed by onshore flow, both at the surface and aloft, into the evening of the fourteenth.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter at milepost 17 on the Glacier Highway measured 3.8 inches of new snow at 0800 on the thirteenth and 5.9 inches of new snow at 0800 on the fourteenth. Analyses indicate that this snowfall occurred on the morning and afternoon of the thirteenth. Lesser amounts fell in town.

CZ_NAME: JUNEAU BOROUGH AND NORTHERN ADMIRALTY ISLAND
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the eleventh a storm force low approached from the south which moved onto the coast near Cape Decision at 981 MB that afternoon. This was rapidly followed by another storm moving to near Sitka at 984 MB on the afternoon of the thirteenth. This center moved northward into the interior on the morning of the fourteenth followed by onshore flow, both at the surface and aloft, into the evening of the fourteenth.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong arctic outflow immediately followed a snow event. Juneau airport had a measured gust of 66 MPH at 1630 on the thirteenth. Sheep Mountain had 115 MPH sustained and a measured gust of 154 MPH at 1630. The Federal building downtown had a measured gust of 75 MPH at 2220. Mayflower Island observed a peak wind of 70 mph at 2033.

CZ_NAME: DIXON ENTRANCE TO CAPE DECISION COASTAL AREA
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the eleventh a storm force low approached from the south which moved onto the coast near Cape Decision at 981 MB that afternoon. This was rapidly followed by another storm moving to near Sitka at 984 MB on the afternoon of the thirteenth. This center moved northward into the interior on the morning of the fourteenth followed by onshore flow, both at the surface and aloft, into the evening of the fourteenth.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The cooperative observer in Craig observed a measured gust of 77 MPH at 1325 on the thirteenth as the power failed in Craig. Hydaburg had a measured gust of 72 mph on the thirteenth.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface low moved along the Southwest coast of Alaska toward the Kenai|Peninsula on the 4th of March. On the morning of the 5th, a strong weather front associated with this low approached the south central coast of Alaska. Wind gusts increased through the morning in the Portage Valley and Turnagain Arm to 60 to 80 mph. The winds in the Southeast Prince William Sound also picked up to 82 mph as the front approached. The winds diminished through the evening of the 5th after the front moved through.
Cold northerly winds in combination with light snowfall created blizzard conditions along the north side of the Alaska Peninsula.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Visibility dropped to a quarter mile or less in blowing snow as winds peaked at over 70 mph in King Cove and the Cold Bay area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the eleventh a storm force low approached from the south which moved onto the coast near Cape Decision at 981 MB that afternoon. This was rapidly followed by another storm moving to near Sitka at 984 MB on the afternoon of the thirteenth. This center moved northward into the interior on the morning of the fourteenth followed by onshore flow, both at the surface and aloft, into the evening of the fourteenth.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The cooperative observer at Hoonah measured 8.4 inches of new snow for twenty-four hours ending at 0800 on the fourteenth. Analyses indicted that most of the new snow fell on the evening of the thirteenth. A trained spotter at Tenakee Springs measured 5.5 inches for the thirteenth.

CZ_NAME: CAPE FAIRWEATHER TO CAPE SUCKLING COASTAL AREA
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Arctic air along the eastern Gulf coast modified on the sixth as a moderate low pressure system redeveloped near Middleton Island. This system caused warm moist air to rise over the Yakutat forelands on the night of the seventh.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Yakutat measured 9 inches of new snow for twelve hours ending at 0400 on the eighth.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the eleventh a storm force low approached from the south which moved onto the coast near Cape Decision at 981 MB that afternoon. This was rapidly followed by another storm moving to near Sitka at 984 MB on the afternoon of the thirteenth. This center moved northward into the interior on the morning of the fourteenth followed by onshore flow, both at the surface and aloft, into the evening of the fourteenth.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter in downtown Haines measured 7.2 inches of new snow between 0900 to 1615 on the twelfth with heavy snow continuing. Haines customs measured 16.0 inches of new snow for twenty-four hours ending at 0800 on the thirteenth.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the eleventh a storm force low approached from the south which moved onto the coast near Cape Decision at 981 MB that afternoon. This was rapidly followed by another storm moving to near Sitka at 984 MB on the afternoon of the thirteenth. This center moved northward into the interior on the morning of the fourteenth followed by onshore flow, both at the surface and aloft, into the evening of the fourteenth.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Glacier Bay cooperative observer measured 11.0 inches of new snow for twenty-four hours ending at 0800 on the fourteenth. Surface observations at the Gustavus airport indicated that the new snow fell on the afternoon and evening of the thirteenth.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense storm moved across the Aleutians into the eastern Bering Sea March 28th and 29th. Strong wind and snow ahead of the associated front produced blizzard conditions across the Pribilof Islands and along the Bering Sea coast March 29th.

An intense storm moved across the Aleutians into the eastern Bering Sea March 28th and 29th. Strong wind and snow ahead of the associated front produced blizzard conditions across the Pribilof Islands and along the Bering Sea coast March 29th.

A Gulf of Alaska low moved into Western Prince William Sound March 18th. the strong east southeast flow resulted in extremely heavy snow in northwest Prince William Sound. Whittier report 40 inches of snow between 8 p.m. on the e18th through 9 a.m. on the 19th of March.

A weather front associated with a strong low over the central Aleutians brought snow and gusty winds to the Pribilof Islands. Visibilities of a quarter mile with wind gusts to 42 knots occurred in the afternoon and evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Visibilities of a quarter mile with wind gusts to 42 knots occurred in the afternoon and evening.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong pressure gradient between a Bering Sea high and a storm in the north Pacific produced strong northwest wind. Snow and blowing snow in King Cove produced a blizzard in that community.
High winds recorded to 76 mph occured at the Whittier access road as a frontal system pushed through the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust was recorded to 76 mph at the Whittier Access Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: North winds blowing off the ice sheet over the northern Bering Sea created blizzard conditions on Saint Paul Island.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Visibility dropped to a quarter mile with north winds gusting as high as 42 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Cold northerly flow on the backside of a low pressure system over the central Aleutians produced gusty winds to 60 mph. Combined with fresh snowfall the winds produced blizzard conditions across the western Aleutians.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts to 60 mph caused areas of blowing snow to frequently reduce visibilities to a quarter mile or less across the Western Aleutians.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A major maritime storm slowly weakened as it moved along the southern Gulf of Alaska on new year's day through the second. Arctic air over the Panhandle slowly retreated as this low forced warm moist air over the region though the forth.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Haines customs observed 11.0 inches of new snow for twenty-four hours ending at 0700 on the second. At 1700 a trained spotter in town at Haines measured 7.5 inches of new snow between 0900 and 1700 on the second. Haines customs measured 16.8 inches of new snow for twenty-four hours ending at 0830 on the third.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A major maritime storm slowly weakened as it moved along the southern Gulf of Alaska on new year's day through the second. Arctic air over the Panhandle slowly retreated as this low forced warm moist air over the region though the forth.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Skagway cooperative observer measured 8.0 inches of new snow that fell overnight ending on the morning of the third.

CZ_NAME: SALISBURY SOUND TO CAPE FAIRWEATHER COASTAL AREA
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A gale force low moved into the eastern Gulf of Alaska on the evening of 12/23. A front moving onshore brought brief heavy snow to the coast near Cross Sound.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Pelican cooperative observer measured 7.4 inches of new snowfall for 24 hours ending at 0800 AKST 12/24. Analyses showed that the snow fell between midnight and 0600 early on the morning of the 12/24.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved rapidly from west to east across the Panhandle on the night of 11/14. Heavy snow showers developed over the Haines area late that night and in the early morning hours of 11/15. Haines Customs received 6 inches of new snow in approximately six hours in early on Saturday morning, 11/15.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Haines Customs measured 6.0 inches of new snowfall for 24 hours ending at 0800 AKST 11/15. Analyses showed that the snow began around midnight and was heavy by 0400 AKST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong Bering Sea storm produced snow and wind over the Kuskokwim Delta resulting in blizzard conditions the evening of January 26th.
A stalled cold front over south central Alaska dumped from 14 to 22 inches of snow over the region. This event was the third heaviest one day snowfall for Anchorage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Chulitna reported 17 inches of snow for the storm total.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High Pressure over the north Chukchi Sea shifted slightly to produce strong winds at Point Hope which resulted in blizzard conditions the night of the 19th through the evening of the 20th. One report from an airplane pilot landing there was that no one would travel from the village to the airstrip to assist him in tying down the plane, due to the strong winds and blowing snow hampering travel to the airstrip. AWOS Wind speeds gusted between 50 and 60 mph before the AWOS stopped reporting on the evening of the 19th.

CZ_NAME: N. BROOKS RNG E OF COLVILLE R
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong high pressure with a cold airmass over the North Slope and a moderate pressure gradient across this area and through Brooks Range passes created occasional Extreme Wind Chills during the 19th through 24th.|Lowest Wind Chills reported:|Zone 205 - The few reporting sites did not show low wind chills, but further south over the zone, and in passes, low wind chills were likely observed based on similar conditions in zone 206.|Zone 206 - Anaktuvuk Pass AWOS. Lowest Wind Chill -65 F; West Kuparuk Mesonet site -62 F.
Blizzard Conditions were observed at Point Hope briefly on the 24th, as strong winds created reduced visibilities in blowing snow.

A low pressure center along with a strong warm front moved north over southwest Alaska on the 30th, providing overrunning moisture for heavy snow, particularly against the southern Nulato Hills and other south and southeast facing slopes.  Later on the 30th, warm enough air caused the snow to change to freezing rain in some locations, though amounts are unknown and likely below warning criteria. |Zone 214: Snowfall began on Saturday and continued into the night Saturday night.  Low visibility at a number of stations in the area such as St. Mary's, Scammon Bay and Mountain Village, suggest heavy snowfall fell, as the temperatures remained below freezing through Saturday morning. Based on Bethel's snowfall of 10 inches for the event, it is likely a similar amount fell over portions of zone 214.|Zone 215: From observations of visibility, temperature, and precipitation amounts and occurrences from Russian Mission, Holy Cross, and Anvik, the region received long durations of snowfall, likely heavy snowfall in low visibility. Based on snowfall at McGrath (6.4 inches) and Bethel (10 inches), it is likely that 6 to 8 inches fell over the southern portion of this zone...including the southern Nulato Hills.

Event Continued from February. Strong high pressure over the North Slope and moderate low pressure over the Gulf of Alaska created moderate winds near the Alaska-Canada border, and with cold temperatures this resulted in a period of Extreme Wind Chill, as reported by the Remote Automated Weather Station at Eagle, where the Wind Chill reached -70 F. Sustained Wind speeds were 20 to 30 mph during the event.

A 983 mb low pressure center moved north to the central Bering Sea on the 31st, and an associated occlusion moved over southwest Alaska with the low eventually weakening north of the Seward Peninsula on the afternoon of the 1st. A low pressure center along with a strong warm front moved north over southwest Alaska on the 30th, providing overrunning moisture for heavy snow, particularly against the southern Nulato Hills and other south and southeast facing slopes.  Later on the 30th, warm enough air caused the snow to change to freezing rain in some locations, though amounts are unknown and likely below warning criteria. |Zone 214: Snowfall began on Saturday and continued into the night Saturday night.  Low visibility at a number of stations in the area such as St. Mary's, Scammon Bay and Mountain Village, suggest heavy snowfall fell, as the temperatures remained below freezing through Saturday morning. Based on Bethel's snowfall of 10 inches for the event, it is likely a similar amount fell over portions of zone 214.|Zone 215: From observations of visibility, temperature, and precipitation amounts and occurrences from Russian Mission, Holy Cross, and Anvik, the region received long durations of snowfall, likely heavy snowfall in low visibility. Based on snowfall at McGrath (6.4 inches) and Bethel (10 inches), it is likely that 6 to 8 inches fell over the southern portion of this zone...including the southern Nulato Hills.

Rapid storm development occurred over the central Gulf on the morning of the twenty-fifth. This system intensified as a low center at 982 MB just off Sitka on the afternoon of the twenty-fifth. Arctic high pressure built over the central interior of Alaska that night as the surface low stalled over the panhandle then formed two centers - one well inland and one over the eastern Gulf. The Gulf low moved slowly while weakening to the Queen Charlotte Islands on the evening of the twenty-sixth as arctic air moved into the Panhandle from the north. Strong arctic high pressure built into the Yukon on the night of the twenty-sixth and persisted through the twenty-seventh.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Hoonah cooperative observer measured 9.7 inches of new snow for the twenty-four hours ending at 0800 on the twenty-sixth. Analyses and nearby surface observations indicated that the new snow fell between 0900 and 2100 on the twenty-fifth.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper low near the Bering Strait pushed into northwest Alaska.  This caused a strong cold front to cross the Alaska Range during the afternoon of the 24th.  The cold front stalled out across the area and cold air behind the front combined with significant moisture from the south to produce a period of heavy snow across the Alaska Range. Although significant snow was observed across the lower elevations, the greatest snowfall totals were observed above 2500 feet. 24 inches of snow as observed at the Trims DOT Camp, and 12 inches of snow was observed at Mile Post 230 on the Parks Highway.

CZ_NAME: EASTERN BARANOF ISLAND AND SOUTHERN ADMIRALTY ISLAND
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A 1046 MB high cell over the Yukon forced the arctic front south into Dixon Entrance. Warm moist air from the Gulf moved aloft over the front for the southern third of the Panhandle on 2/8. Due to an intense change in the upper flow pattern, the high moved southeast to Alberta while a 974 MB low developed in the eastern Gulf on 2/9 which caused heavy snow. While the snow continued, strong pressure gradients developed for gusty winds which diminished later on 2/10. Moisture continued to move aloft over the front with the heavy snow continuing on 2/10 into 2/11. A few areas continued with snow on 2/12.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Hidden Falls hatchery COOP observer measured 13.0 inches of new snow for twenty-four hours ending at 1300 on 2/9 and 5.0 inches at 1300 on 2/10. Compared to the hourly observations at Angoon, Heavy snow began around 0600 on 2/9.

This low then rapidly developed to 982 millibars off Yakutat on the morning of 3/23 and then weakened rapidly on the afternoon of 3/23.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sitka airport ASOS observed a peak wind of 43 knots on 3/22. The Cape Decision CMAN site observed gusts to 73 knots on the afternoon of 3/22.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A 983 mb low pressure center moved north to the central Bering Sea on the 31st, and an associated occlusion moved over southwest Alaska with the low eventually weakening north of the Seward Peninsula on the afternoon of the 1st. This system brought heavy snow and freezing rain to the eastern and northern shores of Norton Sound the night of the 31st and morning of the 1st. |Zone 211 - On the 31st, 3.8 inches of snow fell at Nome. In addition, St. Michael experienced some freezing rain, though below warning criteria.|Zone 214 - Rain fell across the zone, freezing on contact with roads and  streets. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stalled cold front over south central Alaska dumped from 14 to 22 inches of snow over the region. This event was the third heaviest one day snowfall for Anchorage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wasilla reported 12 inches, Eagle River reported 14.3 inches, and the Anchorage area reported from 17.2 to 22 inches of storm total snow.

A well developed low pressure system moved into the Gulf of Alaska and pushed against the high pressure to the Northeast, tightening the pressure gradient over the Southern Coast. The orientation of the pressure gradient was ideal for channeled winds through the Portage Valley and Turnagain Arm, and as the night progressed the wind speeds increased. At 8:20 P.M. the winds were gusting to 72 mph and continued to do so, off and on through the morning. In the morning the wind speeds reached their peak at 85 MPH, just after 5:00 A.M. and then tapered off through the mid-morning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The swing arm at the entrance to the Whittier tunnel was broken off by the high wind in Bear Valley at the tunnel staging area.

A strong front moved to the the Gulf of Alaska coast producing a brief period of strong wind along Turnagain Arm.

A strong upper level low near the Bering Strait brought a period of strong westerly flow aloft across the Western Alaska Range. The strong westerly flow aloft combined with a strong area of low pressure that tracked across interior Alaska to produce heavy snow from the evening of the 13th through the afternoon of the 14th.  Summit Lake observed 17 inches of snow.
Very unstable atmospheric conditions in southwest Alaska produced severe thunderstorms in the Bethel area the evening of July 5th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Kipnuk reported gusts to 55 mph. Estimate gusts exceeded 50 KT.

CZ_NAME: WRN TANANA VLY WRN YUKON VLY
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An ice jam formed downstream of Old Minto on the Tanana River on the 8th. at 3AM ADT on the 9th, flooding commenced at the Tanana Chiefs Corporation Old Minto Family Recovery Camp. The five Tanana Chiefs employees were evacuated by Army helicopter early on the morning of the 9th. At 3am on the morning of the 9th a woman was stranded on a barge that was stuck in the ice jam near the Tolovana River at the confluence with the Tanana River. An Army helicopter rescued her. The roadhouse the woman owned with her husband at the mouth of the Tolovana was inundated with 4 feet of running water. Ice chunks the size of large trucks moved in around the structures. This is the first time anyone can recall flooding in this location (over 90 yrs). This has been historically known as a spot that does not flood. Damage amount is unknown as of report deadline.

An ice jams on the Kwigkuk River the evening of the 24th caused flooding of portions of the village...including the road to the airport and low-lying areas of the village. Water levels fell quickly on the night of the 25th as the ice began running in the river. Property damage amount not available by report deadline.

On the 12th, an ice jam formed downstream of the village of Kobuk, just upstream of Shungnak, and the river began rising overnight, moving into creeks and lakes around the village on the morning of the 13th, and inundating the lower parts of the village..then moving up to affect the most of the structures and airport through the afternoon of the 13th. On the night of the 13th the ice jam went out and water levels decreased rapidly with most streets dry by the morning of the 14th. Damage occurred to the main road through the portion of town that was flooded. No homes were damage. Property Damage amount is estimated at $5000 for road repair.

On the 12th, an ice jam downstream of the village of Buckland backed up water to flood portions of the village. The ice jam released on the 13th and water levels dropped to within the river bank. The road to uptown, and the dump, were flooded as well as water moving along streets. Property damage is estimated at $5000 for road repair.

An ice jam formed near Piledriver Slough north of Salcha on the evening of the 4th, causing water to rise in Piledriver Slough and flood several homes. a few hours later another jam formed upstream causing water to rise in the Starkeyville subdivision. On the morning of May 6th, the Fairbanks North Star Borough Emergency Services and Salcha Rescue evacuated approximately 8 families (40 people) from the Starkeyville subdivision on the Tanana River about 1 mile downstream of the confluence of the Salcha River. By the morning of the 8th the ice jams had dislodged and river water started flowing again allowing river water levels to recede below bankfull. Property Damage amount is unknown as of report deadline.

An ice jam formed near Piledriver Slough north of Salcha on the evening of the 4th, causing water to rise in Piledriver Slough and flood several homes. a few hours later another jam formed upstream causing water to rise in the Starkeyville subdivision. On the morning of May 6th, the Fairbanks North Star Borough Emergency Services and Salcha Rescue evacuated approximately 8 families (40 people) from the Starkeyville subdivision on the Tanana River about 1 mile downstream of the confluence of the Salcha River. By the morning of the 8th the ice jams had dislodged and river water started flowing again allowing river water levels to recede below bankfull. Property Damage amount is unknown as of report deadline.

A 980 mb low pressure center moved counter-clockwise across the southern Beaufort Sea during 29th through Aug 1st, bringing several periods of  west winds 30 to 50 mph across the Arctic Coast of Alaska. This caused a combination of high surf and elevated ocean water levels which destroyed the seawall and threatened 3 structures at the village of Wainwright on the western coast, and damaged the seawall and half of the airstrip at the village of Kaktovik, on the far eastern coast. On Aug 1, a father and his 13-year-old son in an open skiff perished in Camden Bay under strong winds as they were boating from their camp to Kaktovik. Property Damage Estimated at $7 million for Wainwright Seawall; $800,000 for Kaktovik Seawall and Airstrip. Event continued into August.
